frameworks.name: higher-order theory of consciousness
frameworks.one_line: phenomenal experience depends on higher-order states representing first-order content

frameworks.name: ambitious higher-order theory
frameworks.one_line: higher-order states alone can suffice for phenomenology without first-order representations

frameworks.name: first-order theory of consciousness
frameworks.one_line: conscious experience constituted by first-order perceptual representations

frameworks.name: recurrent feedback loop to V1
frameworks.one_line: V1 recurrent processing as proposed neural correlate of first-order content

core_question: Can subjects have conscious experiences when the relevant first-order perceptual representations are absent or too weak to support them?
key_assumptions: empirical cases can adjudicate between first-order and higher-order theories
first-order content is dissociable from reported phenomenology
manipulations: examination of empirical cases where first-order representations are missing or weak
contrast of theoretical predictions across first-order and higher-order accounts
key_findings: subjects appear to enjoy conscious experience without adequate first-order representations
V1 recurrent feedback loops are unlikely to be the neural correlate of conscious first-order content
first-order theories struggle to account for phenomenology in these cases
interpretations: an ambitious higher-order approach is the simplest theory accounting for all cases at face value
alternatively, phenomenology may be jointly determined by first-order and higher-order states
